title: The lesser Key of Solomon, Goetia, the book of evil spirits : $b contains two hundred diagrams and seals for invocation and convocation of spirits, necromancy, witchcraft and black art
author: Unknown Author
summary: The Lesser Key of Solomon: Goetia presents a translated grimoire of ceremonial magic, listing invocations, seals, diagrams, and rites for summoning spirits. The editor frames the text with Rosicrucian prefaces, Latin and Greek invocations, and theoretical commentary on magical phenomena as provoked brain-states. It blends archaic names, divine names, and ritual formulas (the Bornless One) with editorial notes invoking historical occult figures and controversies. Practical ceremonial elements—circles, names, perfumes, symbols—are described alongside philosophical interpretations linking Qabalah, Akasa, and psychological mechanisms. The work serves both as a manual for talismanic operations and a speculative defense of magical efficacy and ritual theory.

characters.name: King Solomon
characters.personality: authoritative archetypal magician and king, invoked as originator of the grimoire
characters.lifespan: legendary figure; presented as source of the texts, no narrative fate given

characters.name: G. H. Fra. D.D.C.F.
characters.personality: learned, devoted Rosicrucian translator who began the edition
characters.lifespan: described as having succumbed to assaults by occult forces; no further fate narrated

characters.name: Secret Chief (Rosicrucian Order)
characters.personality: authority figure who ordered the translation and issuance
characters.lifespan: institutional; acts as commissioning power in the preface

characters.name: Mr. Aleister Crowley
characters.age: adult (referred to as poet and magician)
characters.personality: skilled student of magical lore and kabbalist, called to complete the work
characters.lifespan: invited to complete the edition; no further fate detailed in the excerpt

characters.name: Mr. A. E. Waite
characters.personality: critic referenced in a footnote regarding Rosicrucian authenticity
characters.lifespan: mentioned only in critical commentary; no personal fate given

characters.name: L. W. de Laurence
characters.personality: editor/publisher presenting the edition and protective copyright notices
characters.lifespan: publisher of the volume; no narrative fate

characters.name: Mosheh (Mosheh Thy Prophet)
characters.personality: prophetic voice invoked in the Preliminary Invocation, authoritative spiritual figure
characters.lifespan: presented as a prophetic persona; no further narrative fate

characters.name: Osorronophris
characters.personality: a divine-born name invoked as the Bornless One, ineffable and terrible
characters.lifespan: divine, eternal; invoked repeatedly rather than having a narrative fate

characters.name: S.V.A.
characters.personality: named as an evil adept or superior involved in occult intrigue
characters.lifespan: mentioned as having imposed upon the translator; fate unclear

characters.name: H.
characters.personality: another adept or occult figure implicated in the preface controversy
characters.lifespan: referenced only in relation to the translator's disappearance; no fate given

characters.name: R.R.
characters.personality: mysterious person appointed to complete secret work, mentioned briefly
characters.lifespan: named as a confidential successor; no narrative fate

characters.name: D.D.C.F.
characters.personality: abbreviated persona representing the translator's title or order
characters.lifespan: mentioned in preface context; no narrative fate

characters.name: Cimieries
characters.personality: named spirit exemplar used in the text to illustrate mental faculties
characters.lifespan: presented as a spirit corresponding to a mental function; no fate

characters.name: The Four Great Princes
characters.personality: grouped occult entities acting against the translator in the anecdote
characters.lifespan: collective antagonists in the preface anecdote; no narrative fate
